tasks.chapterTitle: Project Overview and Process of Science

messages.content:
Based on the provided document, here is a summary of the **Engaging Adults in Science and Energy (EASE)** project:

### **Project Overview**
*   **Duration & Support:** A five-year Penn State initiative (2012–2016) supported by a National Science Foundation STEM grant (p. 1).
*   **Target Audience:** Adults living in rural Pennsylvania communities experiencing significant natural gas exploration and development (p. 1).
*   **Core Objective:** To empower citizens to engage in informed, civil dialogue regarding contentious energy topics by improving their scientific understanding and communication skills (p. 1).

### **Curriculum Structure**
The program utilized a ten-week educational structure that integrated field experiences with a curriculum covering:
*   **Scientific Foundation:** The process of science, energy systems, and geology/seismic testing (p. 1, p. 2, p. 3).
*   **Industry & Environmental Knowledge:** Shale development engineering, pipeline regulation, land use planning, and water resource management (p. 3, p. 4).
*   **Socio-Economic Impacts:** Analyzing the community, social, and economic effects of energy development (p. 1, p. 4, p. 5).
*   **Communication:** Developing skills to facilitate constructive conversations around energy choices (p. 2).

Lesson 4 — Engaging Adults in Science and Energy - Presentation5 slides
Engaging Adults in Science and Energy
  • Overview of the EASE Project (2012-2016)
  • Focusing on energy, science, and community impact

The Marcellus Matters: Engaging Adults in Science and Energy (EASE) project was a five-year initiative funded by the National Science Foundation that concluded in 2016 (p. 1). 

Key points of the program include:

Community Science Volunteer Program: The project offered residents in rural Pennsylvania—a region undergoing significant natural gas development—ten weeks of educational classes combined with field training (p. 1).

Curriculum Focus: The educational framework addressed four primary areas:
1. Energy systems and science (p. 1, p. 2).
2. The scientific process (p. 1, p. 2).
3. Social and economic consequences of energy development (p. 1).
4. Communication strategies to facilitate civil dialogue regarding contentious issues (p. 1, p. 2).

Objective: The ultimate goal was to empower citizens with the knowledge and tools necessary to participate constructively in public discussions regarding energy choices and societal impacts (p. 1).

This document outlines a curriculum or modular guide focused on scientific processes, energy policy, and the technical aspects of shale development. The content is structured as follows:

Core Scientific and Social Foundations (p. 2)
This section covers the fundamental process of science, evaluates energy choices relevant to Pennsylvania and the United States, and provides strategies for engaging in constructive conversations regarding these topics.

Energy Infrastructure and Engineering (p. 3)
This section focuses on the technical and regulatory aspects of the energy industry, specifically addressing geology and seismic testing, the engineering principles behind shale development, and the regulatory frameworks governing pipeline construction and land management.

The provided document outlines a curriculum or report structure organized into several thematic sections concerning shale gas development and its associated impacts.

Key points include:

Technical and Geological Foundations
The initial sections focus on the geophysical aspects of energy extraction, specifically geological surveying and seismic testing, followed by the technical engineering processes required for shale development (p. [S.3]). 

Regulatory and Infrastructure Framework
The text covers the administrative and physical requirements for pipeline systems, addressing the regulatory landscape, construction methodologies, and the physical impacts to land use (p. [S.3]).

Environmental and Social Considerations
The latter sections address the broader implications of these activities, including land use planning and design, the management of water resources, and the downstream impacts on local communities (p. [S.4]).

The provided text outlines the core components of an environmental studies curriculum. The key subject areas covered are as follows:

Environmental Planning and Resources
The curriculum addresses the complexities of land use planning and design (p. 4), alongside the management and conservation of water resources (p. 4).

Socio-Economic Considerations
The framework evaluates the broader implications of human activity by analyzing specific community impacts (p. 4) and the corresponding economic impacts (p. 5) resulting from environmental and developmental decisions.

Licensing and Attribution
All content is provided under a Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-Share Alike 3.0 United States License as established by the UC Davis ChemWiki (p. 4, p. 5).
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: What was the primary underlying goal of the Community Science Volunteer Program (CSV) beyond simply teaching scientific facts?
options: To lobby for specific changes in natural gas regulation.
To equip citizens with the skills necessary to participate in civil dialogue regarding controversial local issues.
To provide professional-level job training for the local pipeline industry.
To prove that local residents were opposed to shale development.
questions.explanation: The text states the program focused on communication skills specifically to 'enhance citizens' abilities to engage in civil dialogue around contentious topics,' rather than taking a political stance or providing job training.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the instructional design of the EASE project?
options: A purely online training curriculum focused on theoretical energy principles.
A lecture-based program focused exclusively on the economic benefits of energy development.
A combination of classroom learning and hands-on field experiences.
A short-term workshop program concentrated on rapid conflict resolution.
questions.explanation: The program provided both 'ten weekly educational classes along with field experiences,' indicating a hybrid approach to learning.

questions.question: Why would the EASE curriculum include modules on both 'Geology' and 'Communication Skills'?
options: To ensure that participants have both the technical understanding of energy systems and the interpersonal skills to discuss them effectively.
Because the geology of Pennsylvania dictates the communication strategies used by state officials.
To fulfill the requirements of the National Science Foundation, which mandates arts and science integration.
To teach participants how to use geological data to influence political opinion.
questions.explanation: The curriculum bridged technical scientific understanding (geology, engineering) with social engagement skills (constructive conversations), aiming to enable informed public discourse.

questions.question: The EASE project targeted a specific demographic of adults living in rural Pennsylvania. What was the context behind this target group?
options: They had requested energy to be brought to their remote locations for the first time.
They were experiencing large-scale community changes due to natural gas exploration.
They were specifically interested in pursuing careers in environmental journalism.
They were being trained to act as consultants for energy companies.
questions.explanation: The text identifies the audience as adults living in regions where 'natural gas exploration and development was occurring producing large-scale change within their communities.'

questions.question: Which aspect of the curriculum most directly addresses the 'social and economic' facet of the community changes?
options: Modules 4 and 5 (Geology and Engineering).
Module 1 (Process of Science).
Modules 9 and 10 (Community Impacts and Economic Impacts).
Module 8 (Water Resources).
questions.explanation: Modules 9 and 10 specifically address the social (community) and economic impacts mentioned in the program description.

cards.front: What does the acronym EASE stand for in the context of the Penn State project?
cards.back: Engaging Adults in Science and Energy

cards.front: Which organization supported the EASE project from 2012 to 2016?
What was the primary goal of the Community Science Volunteer (CSV) program?
cards.back: To provide educational classes and field experiences to adults in rural Pennsylvania affected by natural gas development.

cards.front: What are the four core focus areas of the EASE curriculum?
cards.back: Energy systems, the scientific process, social and economic impacts, and communication skills.

cards.front: Why was the curriculum designed to include communication skills?
cards.back: To enhance citizens' abilities to engage in civil dialogue around contentious energy topics.

cards.front: In the EASE curriculum, what subjects are covered in Lessons 1-3?
cards.back: Process of Science, Energy Choices for PA and the US, and Constructive Conversations.

cards.front: What technical and infrastructure topics are addressed in Lessons 4-6?
cards.back: Geology and Seismic Testing, Shale Development Engineering, and Understanding Pipeline Regulation, Construction and Land Impacts.

cards.front: Which environmental topics are specifically covered in Lessons 7 and 8?
cards.back: Land Use Planning and Design (7) and Water Resources and Management (8).

cards.front: What major societal impact is covered in Lesson 9?
What is the final topic addressed in Lesson 10 of the EASE program?

slides.title: Engaging Adults in Science and Energy
bullets: Focusing on energy, science, and community impact
slides.notes: This project supported rural Pennsylvania communities through science-based education during periods of rapid energy development. It aimed to foster informed civil dialogue on complex, contentious regional topics.

slides.title: Core Scientific Literacy
bullets: Foundational energy choices for PA and the US
slides.notes: The curriculum bridged technical knowledge with communication strategies, ensuring residents could analyze energy data and engage effectively with their neighbors.

slides.title: Technical Foundations: Geology and Engineering
bullets: Explaining geology and seismic testing methods
Navigating pipeline regulation and construction impacts
slides.notes: These sessions provided the technical background necessary to understand how natural gas exploration physically reshapes the landscape and impacts land use.

slides.title: Environmental and Community Management
bullets: Land use planning and design strategies
slides.notes: This section addressed the environmental consequences of development, equipping citizens with the knowledge to monitor and contribute to local planning efforts.

slides.title: Closing: Socioeconomic Impacts
bullets: Evaluating the economic implications of development
slides.notes: The final module focused on the economic realities of the energy sector, allowing participants to link scientific and environmental findings to their own local economies.
